title: Teaching the Robot to Judge
subtitle: AI & ML, the black box, and bias

hook.metaphor: Imagine training an apprentice by showing them thousands of past loans — who paid back, who didn't — and saying “learn the pattern, then judge new applicants the same way.” That apprentice is a **machine-learning model**. It can crunch numbers at superhuman speed. But it also quietly absorbs every bias in the examples you showed it — including the ones you'd be ashamed of.
hook.scenario: A lender's shiny new AI model approves loans in milliseconds. Months later, someone notices it quietly rejects applicants from certain postcodes far more often — postcodes that happen to track race. Nobody programmed that rule. So where did it come from?

learn: You don't need a statistics degree for the core idea. **Predictive modelling** means: feed software lots of historical examples, and it learns which borrower characteristics tended to go with **default**. Then it scores a new applicant on those same patterns. It's pattern-matching at scale — a very fast version of the gut-call you made in Level 1.
The superpower is **speed and scale**: a model can assess millions of applicants consistently, 24/7, far faster than any human committee.

The trap is the **'black box'**. Many models are so complex that even their builders can't fully explain *why* a particular person was rejected. That's a problem when someone has a legal right to an explanation — and when the model might be wrong.

The deepest danger is **bias**. A model learns from history — and if history was discriminatory, the model learns to discriminate, *without anyone writing a discriminatory rule*. Our scenario's postcode effect is a classic **proxy**: the model never sees 'race', but it finds a stand-in for it. This is how AI can produce **discriminatory lending** while looking perfectly neutral. Regulators are explicit about this — Australia's ASIC and APRA, and global guidance, expect lenders to manage AI fairness, explainability, and **model risk**.

Which is why the future is **human + algorithm**, not algorithm alone. Software is brilliant at crunching numbers at speed, but it lacks human judgement — it can't weigh a borrower's reputation, a one-off life event, or the instinct of someone who knows the sector. The algorithm proposes; a human governs, questions, and overrides.
bridge: So at work, you can be the person in the room who asks the uncomfortable, essential questions about any AI decision: *What data was this trained on, and was that history fair? Can we explain why it rejected someone? Could it be using a proxy for something it's not allowed to consider?* You don't need to build the model to hold it accountable.

interactions.title: Spot the bias
interactions.prompt: Back to the postcode problem: the AI rejects certain postcodes far more, tracking race, though no one coded that rule. What's the right response?

options.label: Nothing's wrong — the model is just being objective with data.
options.feedback: A model trained on biased history isn't 'objective' — it has laundered old discrimination into a neutral-looking score. Postcode here is a proxy for race. 'The data said so' is not a defence.

options.label: Investigate the training data and the proxy, and put a human review in place.
options.feedback: Exactly. You trace where the bias entered (the historical data), test whether postcode is acting as a proxy, and add human oversight. This is fairness + explainability + model risk management in action.

options.label: Just delete the postcode field and assume it's fixed.
options.feedback: Tempting, but often not enough — models can reconstruct a proxy from other correlated fields. Removing the obvious variable is a start, but you still have to test the outcomes for bias.

interactions.title: Human or algorithm?
interactions.prompt: Who is better suited to each job — the fast pattern-matching algorithm, or the human governing it?

items.label: Judging whether a one-off job loss is a real red flag
Spotting a subtle pattern across thousands of variables
Deciding the model is being unfair and overriding it
Weighing a borrower's reputation and sector instinct
Re-scoring instantly when new data arrives

interactions.title: AI credit, de-jargoned
interactions.prompt: Match each term to its plain-English meaning.

pairs.right: The historical examples a model learns its patterns from
A model whose reasoning is too complex to easily explain
A neutral-looking field that secretly stands in for a forbidden one
The risk that the model itself is wrong, biased, or misused
Being able to say *why* the model made a given decision

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “A credit model just learns patterns from past loans — so it's fast and scalable, but it also inherits history's biases, which is why a human still has to question and govern it.”

checkpoint.q: At its core, a machine-learning credit model works by…
options: Following hand-written rules a lawyer wrote
Learning patterns from historical examples of who did and didn't repay
Asking a human to decide each case
checkpoint.explain: It learns from history which characteristics went with default, then scores new applicants on those patterns.

checkpoint.q: A model rejects a group far more often even though no one coded that rule. This most likely means…
options: The model is perfectly fair and objective
It learned bias from historical data, possibly via a proxy variable
Someone manually entered a discriminatory rule
The model is broken and outputs random results
checkpoint.explain: Models inherit bias from biased history and can use proxies (like postcode for race) without any explicit rule.

options: A model being too slow to use
Not being able to easily explain why the model made a decision
A model that only runs at night
Storing data in a physical black box
checkpoint.explain: Complex models can be accurate yet hard to explain — a real problem when people have a right to know why they were refused.
